sections.heading: Conflict is Not the Enemy
sections.body: Most people avoid conflict. That's a mistake. Healthy conflict (disagreement about ideas, methods, or priorities) leads to better decisions, stronger relationships, and more innovation. The goal isn't to eliminate conflict — it's to prevent it from becoming personal. When two people respectfully disagree, the team wins. When two people fight personally, everyone loses.

sections.heading: The Thomas-Kilmann Conflict Modes
sections.body:
Five ways people handle conflict, each appropriate in different situations:

• **Competing**: Win-lose. Use when quick, decisive action is needed (emergencies).
• **Accommodating**: Yield to the other party. Use when the issue matters more to them than to you.
• **Avoiding**: Sidestep the conflict. Use when the issue is trivial or emotions need to cool.
• **Compromising**: Split the difference. Use when time is limited and both parties have equal power.
• **Collaborating**: Win-win. Use when both perspectives are important and you have time to find a creative solution.

sections.heading: The PEACE Framework for Difficult Conversations
sections.body:
A structured approach for resolving conflicts:

• **Prepare**: Gather facts, understand your own emotions, think about their perspective.
• **Engage**: Start with a neutral opening, not an accusation.
• **Acknowledge**: Listen to their side without interrupting.
• **Clarify**: Summarize what you heard. Find the root cause.
• **Exit**: Agree on next steps and a follow-up.

jargon.definition: The process of finding a peaceful solution to a disagreement. It requires communication, empathy, and willingness to compromise.
Fully concentrating on what the other person is saying, not just waiting for your turn to speak. Includes paraphrasing and asking clarifying questions.
A communication technique that expresses feelings without blame: 'I feel frustrated when deadlines are missed' instead of 'You always miss deadlines.'
A neutral third party facilitates the conversation between conflicting parties. The mediator doesn't decide — they help the parties find their own resolution.

summary: Conflict is healthy when it stays focused on ideas, not people. Use the Thomas-Kilmann modes to choose the right approach for each situation, and the PEACE framework for structuring difficult conversations.
